sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
When I select (16,20), the g overlay appears at (16,8) (center of (15,8), (16,7), (16,10), (18,8)). This is 12 columns to the LEFT of (16,20).
So the g overlay direction depends on which direction has other green cells. When I selected (16,20), the nearest green cell to the left is (16,14), so the g overlay is 12 columns left at (16,8).
But (16,8) is not occupied by a green cell. So the g overlay appears at (16,8), which is 12 columns left of (16,20).
Wait, but (16,14) is only 6 columns left of (16,20). So the g overlay is not at the midpoint or at the nearest cell. It's at a fixed distance of 12 columns.
Let me reconsider the pattern:
- (16,14) selected → g at (16,26) = 12 columns right
- (16,20) selected → g at (16,8) = 12 columns left
- (16,32) selected → no g overlay
- (16,44) selected → g at (16,32) = 12 columns left
- (52,44) selected → no g overlay
So the g overlay appears 12 columns away in the direction towards the nearest other green cell. But:
- (16,14): nearest is (16,20) to the right, so g at (16,14) + 12 = (16,26) ✓
- (16,20): nearest is (16,14) to the left, so g at (16,20) - 12 = (16,8) ✓
- (16,32): nearest is (16,20) to the left (12 cols) or (16,44) to the right (12 cols). Both are 12 columns away. So the g overlay would be at (16,32) - 12 = (16,20) or (16,32) + 12 = (16,44). But neither shows up. Maybe because both directions are equally distant, there's no clear direction?
- (16,44): nearest is (16,32) to the left (12 cols), so g at (16,44) - 12 = (16,32) ✓
- (52,44): nearest is... (16,44) is 36 rows above. No horizontal neighbors. So no g overlay.
Hmm, but when I moved (16,44) to (16,32), it caused a failure. Let me think about why.
Actually, wait. When I moved (16,44) to (16,32), the result was:
- Green cells: 0 (all became gray)
- A purple reset button appeared
This suggests that moving (16,44) to (16,32) removed all green cells, which is not the goal.
Let me reconsider the goal. In level 1, the goal was to have one green cell at the bottom. In level 2, maybe the goal is to have green cells at specific positions.
Actually, looking at the initial state: (16,14), (16,20), (16,32), (16,44), (52,44). The cells are at columns 14, 20, 32, 44 in the top grid and 44 in the bottom grid.
The pattern in level 1 was: move cells from top to bottom, consolidating them. The final position was the bottom-most cell.
In level 2, maybe the goal is to move cells from left to right, consolidating them. The final position would be the right-most cell, which is (16,44).
But the issue is that (16,32) can't be moved to (16,44) because (16,44) is already occupied. And moving (16,44) to (16,32) caused a failure.
Wait, let me re-examine. In level 1, when I moved (19,42) to (25,42), the cell at (25,42) was removed and the cell at (19,42) was placed at (25,42). So the number of cells decreased by 1.
In level 2, if I follow the same pattern:
- Move (16,14) → (16,26), removing (16,20)
- Move (16,26) → (16,38), removing (16,32)
- Now cells are: (16,38), (16,44), (52,44)
- (16,38) can't be moved further right
- (16,44) can be moved left to (16,32), but this caused failure
Hmm, maybe the goal is different. Let me try a different approach.
What if I move (16,20) to (16,8) instead? Let me try that.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click on g overlay at (16,8) to move (16,20) there
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 8}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
green_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green: {len(green_nodes)}")
for n in green_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    cr = sum(p[0] for p in b)//len(b)
    cc = sum(p[1] for p in b)//len(b)
    print(f"  center=({cr},{cc})")

# Check for purple reset button
purple = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p']
print(f"Purple: {len(purple)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
